title: Income Tax Case Procedure Bangladesh: How to Handle NBR Disputes
metaTitle: Income Tax Case Bangladesh: NBR Disputes & Appeals
metaDescription: Received an NBR income tax notice Bangladesh? Learn how to respond, object to assessments, appeal to the Taxes Appellate Tribunal, and protect your rights. Expert guide from Dhaka.
heroIntro: Receiving an income tax notice from the National Board of Revenue (NBR) is a stressful experience — whether you are an individual or a company. An incorrect tax assessment, a penalty notice, or a demand for back taxes can have serious financial and legal consequences if not handled correctly. Understanding the income tax dispute procedure in Bangladesh — and your rights at each stage — is essential to protecting yourself and your business.

sections.h2: Overview: Income Tax Disputes in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Income tax disputes in Bangladesh arise when there is a disagreement between a taxpayer and the National Board of Revenue (NBR) regarding the amount of tax owed, allowable deductions, tax exemptions, or penalties. The Income Tax Act 2023 (replacing the Income Tax Ordinance 1984) governs all income tax proceedings in Bangladesh.

The dispute resolution process in Bangladesh's income tax system follows a multi-stage hierarchy: NBR assessment → Commissioner's objection → Taxes Appellate Tribunal → High Court Division. A tax lawyer in Dhaka can represent you at every stage to protect your financial interests.

sections.h2: Types of NBR Income Tax Notices You May Receive
sections.content:
The NBR issues several types of income tax notices in Bangladesh:

  • Section 163 Notice (return submission): Requiring you to submit an income tax return if you have not done so
  • Section 176 Notice (audit/assessment): Notifying you that your tax return is being audited and you must produce books of accounts and documents
  • Demand Notice (after assessment): A formal demand for additional tax based on the completed assessment order
  • Penalty Notice: Imposing a penalty for late filing, concealment of income, or failure to deduct TDS
  • TDS Default Notice: Against employers or businesses that failed to deduct and deposit withholding tax
  • Show Cause Notice: Asking you to explain why a penalty or additional tax should not be imposed

Each type of notice requires a specific, timely response. Missing the response deadline can result in an adverse order issued ex parte (without hearing your side).

sections.h2: How to Respond to an Income Tax Notice
sections.content:
When you receive any income tax notice from NBR, the most important step is to respond within the stated deadline. Here is the general approach:

  1. Identify the notice type and deadline. Read the notice carefully. Note the response deadline — typically 15–30 days from the notice date.
  2. Gather all relevant documents. Depending on the notice: income tax returns for the relevant years, audited financial statements, bank statements, evidence of allowable deductions, TDS certificates, and any other documents referenced in the notice.
  3. Draft a written response. Address every point in the notice factually and with supporting documentation. Vague or incomplete responses invite follow-up notices and adverse orders.
  4. Attend the hearing if required. Some notices require a personal or representative appearance before the Inspecting Joint Commissioner or Tax Circle. Your lawyer can attend as your authorised representative.
  5. File the response within the deadline. Late responses are treated as defaults and the tax authority can proceed ex parte.

The NBR conducts tax assessments through the Tax Circle Officer (Tax Commissioner's designated representative). The assessment process involves:

  • Review of your filed return (if filed) or estimation based on available information (if not filed)
  • A notice for production of books of accounts, statements, and relevant documents
  • Examination of your income sources, claimed deductions, and any discrepancies
  • Preliminary findings are communicated and you are given an opportunity to respond (a Show Cause notice)
  • A final assessment order is issued specifying the total income assessed and the tax demand

The assessment order sets out the tax officer's conclusions. If you disagree with the assessment, you have the right to appeal — but this right must be exercised within the specified limitation period, typically 45–90 days from the assessment order date.

sections.h2: Objecting to a Tax Assessment: Filing an Appeal
sections.content:
Under the Income Tax Act 2023, a taxpayer who disagrees with a tax assessment order can file an appeal before the Tax Commissioner (Appeals) — also known as a first appeal:

  1. File a written objection (appeal petition) within the prescribed time limit from the assessment order date
  2. Pay the prescribed amount of disputed tax as a condition of appeal admission (currently one-third or as directed)
  3. Attach supporting documents, accounts, and legal arguments
  4. The Commissioner (Appeals) hears both parties and issues an order

Key grounds for first appeal: calculation errors in the assessment, improper disallowance of legitimate deductions, failure to consider submitted evidence, assessment based on estimated income without basis, and procedural irregularities in the assessment process.

sections.h2: Taxes Appellate Tribunal: Second Level of Appeal
sections.content:
If the first appeal before the Commissioner (Appeals) does not resolve the dispute, the taxpayer can file a further appeal before the Taxes Appellate Tribunal — a quasi-judicial body constituted under the Income Tax Act with independent members.

The Tribunal reviews questions of fact and law. Proceedings are more formal — legal arguments and written submissions are presented, and both parties may be represented by lawyers. The Tribunal's decision is authoritative on factual matters.

Beyond the Tribunal, further appeals on questions of law go to the High Court Division — making it the final stage for legal questions about tax law interpretation.

sections.h2: High Court Division: Final Legal Remedy
sections.content:
Appeals from the Taxes Appellate Tribunal on questions of law go to the High Court Division under Section 160 of the Income Tax Act (previously Section 158 of the Ordinance 1984). The High Court:

  • Considers questions of law — not fresh factual disputes
  • Can set aside, modify, or uphold the Tribunal's decision
  • Issues authoritative rulings that bind tax authorities on the legal question

Additionally, a writ petition before the High Court Division under Article 102 of the Constitution is available where the NBR has acted without jurisdiction, violated fundamental rights, or acted in breach of natural justice. This is a powerful and often faster remedy in clear cases.

An experienced Supreme Court advocate handles High Court tax appeals and writ petitions in income tax cases.

sections.h2: Common Grounds for Successful Tax Appeals
sections.content:
Experienced tax lawyers in Bangladesh know which arguments are most effective in the appeals process:

  • Addition without basis: The tax officer added income without any documentary or credible evidence — a common and successful ground for appeal
  • Improper disallowance of deductions: Legitimate business expenses, depreciation, or bad debts wrongfully disallowed
  • Double taxation: The same income taxed twice — at withholding and again at assessment
  • Statute of limitations: Assessment issued after the limitation period expired
  • Procedural failures: Assessment made without proper notice, hearing denied, or fair opportunity to present evidence not given
  • Incorrect application of tax rate or exemption: Wrong tax bracket applied or available exemption not granted

faqs.question: What should I do if I receive an income tax notice from NBR in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Read the notice carefully, note the response deadline, gather all relevant financial documents, and consult a tax lawyer immediately. File a written response within the deadline. Missing the deadline allows the tax authority to proceed ex parte, usually resulting in an adverse assessment.

faqs.question: Can I appeal against a NBR income tax assessment in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. File a first appeal before the Commissioner (Appeals) within the limitation period from the assessment order date. Pay the prescribed portion of the disputed tax as a condition of admission. A tax lawyer can identify the strongest grounds for your appeal.

faqs.question: What is the Taxes Appellate Tribunal in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: The Taxes Appellate Tribunal is a quasi-judicial appellate body that hears appeals from Commissioner (Appeals) decisions in income tax cases. It is independent of the NBR and has the authority to modify or set aside tax assessments. Further appeals from its decisions go to the High Court Division on questions of law.

faqs.question: Can I go to the High Court against an NBR income tax decision?
faqs.answer: Yes — through two routes. First, an appeal on a question of law from the Taxes Appellate Tribunal. Second, a writ petition under Article 102 of the Constitution if the NBR acted without jurisdiction or violated your legal rights. The High Court can set aside unlawful tax demands.

faqs.question: How long does an income tax appeal take in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: First appeal (Commissioner Appeals): 3–12 months. Taxes Appellate Tribunal: 1–3 years. High Court appeal: 2–5+ years. These are estimates — actual timelines depend on case complexity and court scheduling. An experienced tax lawyer can accelerate hearings through proper case management.
